title: Vanilla cupcakes
subtitle: Light vanilla sponge baked in paper cases, topped with vanilla buttercream.
excerpt: Vanilla cupcakes are a small Victoria sponge baked individually in paper cases. The sponge is light and buttery with a rounded top that the buttercream sits on. The ratio of butter to sugar to eggs to flour is equal weight, the same as any Victoria sponge. Makes 12.
sourceNotes: The Victoria sponge ratio (equal weights of fat, sugar, eggs, and flour) has been the British baking standard since the mid-nineteenth century. Individual paper-cased versions of this cake have been popular at British bake sales and tea rooms throughout the twentieth century. The American 'cupcake' format with piped frosting became widely popular in Britain from the late 1990s onwards, partly through the influence of American TV and the opening of shops like the Hummingbird Bakery in London in 2004. This recipe uses the classic British sponge method with an American-style piped buttercream topping.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted cupcakes in a single layer, then transfer to a bag. Defrost at room temperature for an hour, then frost.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles well. Bake in two batches if you only have one muffin tin.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponges keep well for two days in an airtight tin. The buttercream can be made a day ahead and kept at room temperature in a bowl covered with cling film; beat briefly before using.

recipeTools.notes: 12-hole muffin tin lined with paper cases.
Or a hand mixer. For both the sponge and the buttercream.
For a swirled buttercream top. A teaspoon works just as well for a simpler finish.
A star nozzle gives the classic swirl.

content.text: Vanilla cupcakes are a Victoria sponge in individual form. The equal-weight method (same weight of butter, sugar, eggs, and flour) is the simplest route to a reliable light sponge, and it scales precisely by the weight of the eggs you have.
The buttercream is beaten butter, icing sugar, a splash of milk, and vanilla. Beat it for longer than you think necessary; pale, fluffy buttercream needs at least 5 minutes of mixing to become light.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Line a 12-hole muffin tin with paper cases.
Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and very fluffy, about 3 to 4 minutes. Add {{vanilla-extract}}.
Add {{eggs}} one at a time, beating well between each. If the mixture curdles, add a tablespoon of flour to bring it back together.
Add {{self-raising-flour}} to the bowl in two additions, alternating with {{whole-milk}}. Fold each addition in gently until just combined.

Divide the batter evenly between the 12 cases, filling each about two thirds full. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until risen, pale golden, and springy to the touch. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack.

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} alone on high speed for 2 minutes until very pale. Add {{icing-sugar}} in three additions, beating for 1 minute between each. Add {{vanilla-extract}} and {{whole-milk}} and beat for a further 3 minutes until light and fluffy.
Pipe or spoon buttercream onto each cooled cupcake. If piping, use a star nozzle and pipe in a swirl from the outside in. If spooning, use the back of the spoon to create a small peak.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Cupcakes are straightforward but have a few reliable failure points.

items.symptom: Peaked and domed tops rather than flat.
items.cause: Oven too hot, or too much baking powder in self-raising flour.
items.fix: Lower the temperature by 10 degrees and check that the flour is self-raising, not plain. A slightly domed top is fine for a buttercream swirl; a sharply peaked top suggests over-leavening.
Refrigerate the bowl for 10 minutes, then beat again. Buttercream needs cool, soft butter, not warm melting butter.
